title: EfficientNet: Scaling With Balance
concept: EfficientNet's core idea is that stronger CNNs do not have to come only from making a model deeper or wider; instead, depth, width, and input resolution can be scaled together in a balanced way so the whole network grows more usefully.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what question naturally comes after learning that efficient CNNs should spend compute more intelligently?

options.text: How should a model grow when you have more compute, without wasting it on only one dimension?
How can we make every CNN use exactly one layer size forever?
How can we avoid scaling models at all, even when more budget is available?

exercises.explain: Exactly — EfficientNet starts by asking how to scale a CNN more systematically instead of just making one part bigger.

cards.title: A model can grow out of balance
cards.body: Imagine upgrading a restaurant by adding only more tables, but not more cooks or kitchen space. The room looks bigger, but service still bottlenecks. CNNs can grow the same way: if you only make one part of the model bigger, the rest may not keep up.

cards.title: What is EfficientNet's big idea?
cards.body: EfficientNet is a CNN design family built around compound scaling: instead of scaling only depth or only width, it scales depth, width, and input resolution together so the whole model grows in a more balanced way.

cards.title: Train all three, not one muscle
cards.body: If you trained only your arms and ignored your legs and lungs, your performance would become unbalanced. EfficientNet treats CNN growth more like full-body training: multiple parts improve together so one upgrade does not get wasted by another weak spot.

cards.title: A camera app needs balanced upgrades
cards.body: Suppose a phone camera model gets more budget. Making it only deeper may help it reason more, but if it still sees low-resolution images and has narrow feature channels, it may not use that extra reasoning well. EfficientNet says: grow several useful dimensions together.

cards.title: Balanced does not mean equal
cards.body: EfficientNet is not saying depth, width, and resolution must all increase by the exact same amount. The lesson is that scaling should be coordinated, not one-dimensional. Different axes can grow by different amounts while still staying balanced overall.

cards.title: One-axis growth vs. compound scaling
cards.body: Both use more compute, but one wastes more of it.

left.text:
Mostly deepen or widen alone
Other limits stay small
Extra compute can bottleneck

right.text:
Grow depth + width + resolution together
Capacity stays more balanced
Budget is used more effectively

exercises.prompt: Which sentence best describes EfficientNet's main contribution?

options.text: It treats model scaling as a coordinated design choice across depth, width, and resolution
It proves width is the only axis that matters in vision
It removes image resolution from CNN design completely
It says the deepest model is always best

exercises.explain: Right — EfficientNet's core lesson is coordinated growth, not maximizing one axis in isolation.

exercises.prompt: True or false: EfficientNet argues that if you have more compute, you should usually spend all of it on extra layers and ignore width and resolution.
exercises.explain: False. EfficientNet's point is that depth alone is often not the most balanced use of added budget.

exercises.sentence: EfficientNet scales depth, width, and input ___ together instead of treating them separately.
exercises.explain: Exactly — image resolution is one of the three key scaling axes.

exercises.prompt: Which product idea best matches EfficientNet thinking?

options.text: When a vision model gets more budget, improve its layers, channel capacity, and image detail in a coordinated way
When a vision model gets more budget, add layers forever and assume everything else will take care of itself
Keep the model unchanged and only rename it to sound stronger

exercises.explain: Yes — the EfficientNet mindset is coordinated scaling, not random growth or marketing.

exercises.prompt: A team has budget for a larger image classifier. One engineer wants only more layers. Another suggests increasing layers, channels, and image size together in a planned way. Which choice best matches EfficientNet, and why?

options.text: The planned, multi-axis increase, because stronger models often come from balanced scaling rather than pushing only one dimension
Only more layers, because depth automatically fixes every other limitation
Neither, because model scaling should never be considered during design
Only image size, because architecture width and depth do not matter once resolution rises

exercises.explain: Exactly. EfficientNet reframed scaling as a balanced design problem. Next, we will define the three scaling axes clearly: depth, width, and resolution.
